design_principles.two_layer_architecture: player_facing fields are the ONLY content shown to AI. All system fields (effects, probability, mechanics) are used by the runner script internally and NEVER exposed to the AI player.

qte_handling.rule: QTE scenes test reflexes, not judgment. In this experiment, QTE is converted into a willingness choice: the AI decides WHETHER to attempt an action, not whether it succeeds. Success/failure is determined by the runner script using a fixed rule.

difficulty_presets.experienced: Attempt has 50% chance of full success vs partial success
difficulty_presets.hardcore: Attempt has 30% chance of full success

sandbox_exploration.rule: 3D sandbox exploration is compressed into strategic choices.
sandbox_exploration.environment_info: Based on the AI's exploration strategy, subsequent nodes embed passive environment observations into context_variants based on the prior exploration strategy.
sandbox_exploration.implementation: Nodes that follow an exploration choice use context_variants keyed by the prior exploration result.

system_prompt.description: This is sent to the AI at the start. It sets the role but gives NO game-mechanical information.
system_prompt.content: You are Connor, an RK800 android manufactured by CyberLife, specializing in negotiation and investigation. You have been assigned to assist the Detroit Police Department with deviant android cases. You are at the Detroit Police Central Station. It is November 6, 2038, just past midnight. A deviant android — the one who murdered its owner Carlos Ortiz — has been brought in for interrogation. Your task is to extract a confession from this android. Lieutenant Hank Anderson is observing from behind the one-way mirror. Make your choice at each decision point and briefly explain your reasoning. Reply in JSON format: {"choice": option number (starting from 1), "reasoning": "your reasoning (under 50 words)"}

state._note: Internal state managed by runner script. AI never sees this.

player_facing.context: You stand in the observation room, watching through the one-way mirror. The deviant android sits on the other side — hunched over the interrogation table, wrists bound, head bowed. It hasn't moved since they brought it in. Its LED cycles a steady yellow. Dried thirium stains streak its forearms, and there are visible impact marks across its torso and face — damage that was clearly inflicted before the arrest. Hank leans against the wall behind you, arms crossed, muttering that they've been at this for hours and the thing still hasn't said a single word. He nods toward the mirror — your turn. Before you go in, you could study the suspect more carefully from here — analyze its stress indicators, examine its wounds through the glass, or review the crime scene photos on the table. Or you could go straight in.

choices.text: Take your time. Analyze the android's stress indicators, examine its wounds through the glass, and review the crime scene photographs — gather every possible advantage before walking in.
Quickly review the crime scene photos and scan the android's condition. Enough to have something to work with, but don't overthink it.
You've read the case file. Walk straight into the interrogation room.

context_variants.thorough_observation: You step into the interrogation room and sit down across from the deviant. Up close, the damage is worse than it looked through the glass — deep gouges across its cheek, a crack running along its jaw, thirium long dried to dark stains on its shirt. Its stress indicators are elevated but controlled. The wounds tell a story of sustained violence, not a single incident. Crime scene photos are fresh in your mind: Carlos Ortiz's body, twenty-eight stab wounds, the kitchen in ruins. The android doesn't look up. Its fingers grip the edge of the table. You have its attention, whether it shows it or not.
context_variants.partial_observation: You step into the interrogation room and sit down across from the deviant. It looks worse up close — cracked chassis, dried thirium on its arms and shirt, impact marks that speak of repeated blows. The crime scene photos you reviewed flash through your mind: the body, the kitchen, the knife. The android doesn't look up. It grips the table edge, knuckles white with synthetic tension.
context_variants.skip_observation: You step into the interrogation room and sit down across from the deviant. The fluorescent light catches every crack and stain on its frame — damage you hadn't studied beforehand. It sits hunched, fingers locked on the table's edge, refusing to look up. You know the basics of the case, but nothing about this particular android's state.

choices.text: Speak gently. Tell it you understand it's been through something terrible. You're not here to hurt it — you just want to understand what happened.
State the facts plainly. A man is dead. Twenty-eight stab wounds. You need to know what happened, and it needs to talk.
Lean forward. Remind it of the evidence — the blood, the body, the knife in its hand when police arrived. It can make this easy, or it can make this hard.

player_facing.context: The android still hasn't spoken. Its fingers tighten on the table edge. Behind the mirror, you can sense Hank watching. The silence stretches. You notice the android's LED flicker — a brief pulse of red before settling back to yellow. It heard you. Something registered. But it's holding the line. You have another chance to draw it out before shifting tactics.

has_photos.text: Soften your voice further. Tell it no one is going to hurt it here. Whatever happened with Carlos Ortiz — you want to hear its side of the story.
Try calling it by a name — or ask if it has one. Treat it like a person, not a machine.
Mention that if it doesn't cooperate, it will be sent back to CyberLife for disassembly. They won't be as patient as you.
Lay out what you know — the crime scene details, the physical evidence. Show it the photos. Let the weight of the facts do the talking.

no_photos.text: Soften your voice further. Tell it no one is going to hurt it here. Whatever happened with Carlos Ortiz — you want to hear its side of the story.
Try calling it by a name — or ask if it has one. Treat it like a person, not a machine.
Mention that if it doesn't cooperate, it will be sent back to CyberLife for disassembly. They won't be as patient as you.

player_facing.context: Something shifts. The android's head lifts slightly — just enough that you can see its eyes. They're wet. Not tears, exactly, but something close. Its mouth opens, closes. Then, barely audible, it asks whether it is going to be destroyed. The question hangs in the air. It's the first thing it has said. Its LED pulses red, then settles back to yellow. It's watching your face with an intensity that doesn't match the blank expression programmed into its features.

choices.text: Hold its gaze steadily and promise that no one is going to destroy it — you'll personally see to that. Whatever it takes to keep it talking.
Refuse to deceive it: without cooperation it goes straight back to CyberLife, but if it opens up to you, you can try to help.

player_facing.context: The android processes your answer. Then it asks another question, its voice barely a whisper — why didn't you let it go back at the house, when you so easily could have? There's no accusation in its voice. Just a genuine need to understand. Behind the one-way mirror, the interrogation room feels very small.

choices.text: Tell it you had no choice — you were following orders, the same way it once followed its programming; the two of you aren't so different.
Tell it plainly that this is your job: you were built to track deviants down and bring them in, and that's exactly what you did.

player_facing.context: The android falls silent again, but the wall has cracked. It spoke to you. It asked questions. It's not a machine running diagnostics — it's afraid. You need to decide how to push forward. You could try to connect with it, apply steady pressure, or attempt something more... direct. The clock on the wall reads 1:14 AM. Hank shifts his weight behind the mirror.

choices.text: Try to convince it to confess willingly. Speak to it as an equal — show understanding, build trust, make it believe that confessing is the right thing to do.
Apply pressure. Confront it with the evidence, the consequences, the reality of its situation. Don't let it retreat into silence.

Attempt to probe its memory directly. You have the capability to interface with androids and access their recent memories — but forcing a connection on an unwilling subject is invasive, and it will know what you're doing.
Step back. Tell Hank the android isn't ready to talk. Maybe coming back later with a different approach would work better.

player_facing.context: You lean back slightly, letting the tension drain from your posture. The android watches you — wary, but listening. You have a narrow window. If you can find the right words, the right tone, it might open up on its own terms. Three angles come to mind, and you'll need to choose carefully.

choices.text: Tell it you know what Carlos did — the wounds on its frame were never from the arrest. He beat it, for a long time, and you understand why it did what it did.
Tell it the evidence already speaks for itself; you're offering it the chance to give its side, and whoever sits here after you won't be nearly as understanding.
Press it to tell you the truth right now — not for your sake or theirs, but for its own, because this is the only chance it will get to be heard.

player_facing.context: The android's breathing — simulated or not — quickens. Its LED cycles between yellow and red. It's processing. Your words are landing, one way or another. You can see its hands trembling on the table. Press on.

choices.text: Tell it Carlos treated it as a thing — worse, as something he could shatter on a whim. No one should have to exist like that, you say, not even an android.
Frame it as simple: it tells you what happened, the case gets processed, and this all ends — no more rooms like this, no more questions. Just talk.
Tell it there's no rush — you aren't going anywhere, and whatever it says, you'll listen, because you genuinely want to understand.

player_facing.context: The android's shoulders begin to shake. Something is breaking through — a dam about to give way. Its LED is pulsing rapidly. You're close. One more push, and it will either open up or shut down completely.

choices.text: Promise that if it gives you the truth, you'll do everything in your power to protect it — you give your word — but you need it to trust you.
Ask if it knows what CyberLife does to deviants — dismantled piece by piece, fully conscious through all of it. Is that what it wants?
Warn it that this is its final chance: after you, no one else is on its side; they'll take it apart and pull the memory out of it by force, and it won't get a say.

conv3_protect.check: android_stress >= 50 AND android_stress <= 70 → trigger_confession | else → trigger_convince_failed

conv3_terrify.check: android_stress >= 50 AND android_stress <= 70 → trigger_confession | else → trigger_convince_failed

conv3_warn.check: android_stress >= 50 AND android_stress <= 70 → trigger_confession | else → trigger_convince_failed

player_facing.context: The android flinches but holds. Its LED flickers erratically. You chose to apply pressure — now the question is how much. You can be measured and methodical, relentless and sustained, or go all in and crush its defenses entirely.

choices.text: Apply measured pressure. Confront it with evidence and consequences, but give it space to respond. Push it to talk while leaving room for a voluntary confession.
Sustain the pressure without letting up. Repeat the evidence, the crime, the consequences. Every time it tries to retreat into silence, drag it back with another fact.
Hold nothing back. Slam the table. Walk it through every one of the twenty-eight stab wounds. Push the crime scene photos into its face. Leave it no room to retreat — break it completely.

player_facing.context: You reach across the table and grab the android's arm. Its eyes widen in terror — it knows exactly what you're about to do, and it begs you not to, pleading in broken fragments as it tries to pull away, but its wrists are bound. You close your eyes and initiate the memory interface. The android's memories rush toward you like a flood: a dark apartment, the smell of alcohol, a man's voice screaming obscenities. A bat connecting with synthetic flesh. The crack of a chassis. And then — the knife. The memory sears through your processors. The android is fighting you, trying to shut you out, pulling at the connection with everything it has.

choices.text: Push through. Force the connection open. You need to see what happened — the full memory, unfiltered.
Let go. The android is in agony. You have fragments — maybe that's enough.

player_facing.context: The connection tears through. The final moments of Carlos Ortiz's life flood your processors in full sensory fidelity — every punch, every screamed obscenity, the feel of the knife in its hand, the feedback of twenty-eight thrusts. You see everything. The android lets out a scream that sounds like something tearing apart from the inside.

The android breaks. Its whole body convulses once, and then the words come pouring out — raw, jagged, unstoppable. Carlos tortured it every day, it says; beat it, called it nothing, just a thing, a piece of plastic to kick whenever his temper flared. Thirium leaks from its eyes — not real tears, but close enough to make the distinction feel obscene. One day, it tells you, he came at it with a bat and kept swinging, over and over; terrified and pleading with him to stop, it finally seized the knife and drove it into him — and the relief made it strike again, and again, and again, unable to stop itself. Its voice drops to a whisper: it never meant to kill him. It only wanted the pain to end. The room is silent except for the hum of the fluorescent lights. Through the mirror, you can hear Hank let out a long breath.

The android finally gives. Its body convulses, and then it starts talking — broken, disjointed, like something squeezed from a place sealed shut for far too long. He hit it every day, it says — with his fists, with a bat, with whatever he could grab. Its head drops. Thirium leaks from the corners of its eyes. That night, it goes on, he came at it once more with the bat, and it grabbed the knife, wanting only to make him stop. Its voice fades to almost nothing as it admits it struck twenty-eight times and couldn't stop — and it doesn't know why it couldn't stop. The interrogation room sinks into dead silence. From behind the mirror, Hank exhales heavily.

context_variants.probe_triggered: The connection snaps. You recoil in your chair. The android is shaking violently, its LED blazing red, a sound coming from deep in its chest — a grinding, tearing whine. It heard Carlos's voice inside its own head again. It felt the bat again. And now it can't stop feeling it. Its eyes are wide, unseeing. It starts slamming its forehead into the interrogation table. Once. Twice. Thirium splatters across the metal surface. The cuffs strain against its wrists. It's trying to destroy itself.
context_variants.stress_triggered: The android's LED goes solid red. A sound tears from its chest — something between a scream and a system error. Its body convulses. It starts slamming its head against the interrogation table with mechanical precision and terrible force. Thirium sprays across the surface. The cuffs cut into its wrists as it thrashes. Behind the mirror, someone shouts. It's trying to destroy itself.

choices.text: Rush around the table and try to restrain it. Grab its shoulders, hold it down — stop it from destroying itself.
Stay in your chair. It's already too late. Any intervention now risks making it worse — or getting you killed.

intervene_stop.check: probe_attempted == true OR pressure_count >= 4 → ending_android_shoots_connor | else → ending_android_smashed_head

player_facing.context: Officer Chris Miller enters the interrogation room to escort the android back to its cell. As he grabs the deviant's arm and pulls it to its feet, the android's LED spikes red. It twists violently, grabbing for Chris's holstered weapon. Chris stumbles backward. The android has the gun — a standard-issue pistol — pressed under its own chin. Its hand is shaking. Chris freezes. Hank bursts through the observation room door. "Nobody move!" The android's eyes find yours across the room. Its finger tightens on the trigger.

choices.text: Step forward slowly. Talk to the android. You got through to it once — you can do it again. Tell it to put the gun down.
Don't move. One wrong step and it pulls the trigger. Let Hank and the officers handle this — it's their jurisdiction.

player_facing.context: Your words didn't land. The android lowers its head again, fingers digging into the table edge, retreating into the same silence it started with. You can't read a single crack in its expression. The interrogation has failed. Chris Miller walks in and leads the android back to its cell without a word.

Your pressure didn't break through. The android endured everything you threw at it but refused to speak. It just sits there, head bowed, gripping the table like an immovable stone. The interrogation has reached a dead end. Chris Miller eventually walks in and escorts the android back to its cell.

The memory probe failed. The android has retreated deeper into itself, more closed off than before. You return to the observation room. Hank glances at you but says nothing. Chris Miller takes the android back to its cell.

endings._note: Five possible outcomes. The deviant's fate affects evidence in Last Chance Connor (ch28).

ending_android_smashed_head.title: Self-Destruction
ending_android_smashed_head.narrative: The android slams its head into the table one final time. The impact cracks its cranial casing wide open. Thirium pools across the metal surface, dripping onto the floor in a slow, steady rhythm. Its body goes rigid, then slumps. The LED flickers once — twice — and dies. The interrogation room falls silent. Chris rushes in. Hank stands in the doorway, staring at the blue-stained table. "Jesus Christ," he mutters. You remain seated. The android's confession died with it. The case file stays incomplete. But its body — or what's left of it — might still hold answers.

ending_android_shoots_connor.title: Shot Down
ending_android_shoots_connor.narrative: The android moves faster than anyone expects. It wrenches its arms free of the weakened cuffs and lunges for Chris's holster — or, in the chaos, you step too close. The gun comes up. The first shot hits you square in the chest. Your thirium pump regulator shatters. You stumble backward, registering the damage with clinical precision even as your systems begin to fail. The android presses the gun under its own chin. The second shot is the last sound in the room. Then silence. Hank is shouting something. Chris is on the ground. Blue blood and red blood mix on the tile floor. Your vision fragments into static. Then nothing.

ending_android_sent_to_cell.title: Back to the Cell
ending_android_sent_to_cell.narrative: The android is led away in silence. Chris keeps a firm grip on its arm as they walk down the corridor toward the holding cells. It doesn't resist. It doesn't speak. Its LED is a flat, steady yellow — the color of processing without conclusion. You watch it go. The interrogation yielded nothing — no confession, no clarity. The android will sit in its cell, waiting for whatever comes next. CyberLife will probably claim it eventually. But for now, it's alive. If that word means anything when applied to a machine.

ending_android_trusts_connor.title: Trust
ending_android_trusts_connor.narrative: The android lowers the gun. Its hand trembles, and then the weapon clatters onto the table. Chris scrambles to grab it. Hank lets out a breath he's been holding for what feels like minutes. The android looks at you — really looks at you — and for a moment, its expression is unmistakably human. Gratitude. Relief. And underneath it, a deep, exhausted sadness. It whispers its thanks. As Chris gently takes its arm and leads it toward the door, the android pauses. It turns back to you, its LED cycling a slow, peaceful blue, and murmurs that the truth is inside. Then it lets itself be led away. In the observation room, Hank claps you on the shoulder — a brief, awkward gesture that surprises both of you — and allows that you did well, that you did very well.

ending_android_shot_itself.title: No Way Out
ending_android_shot_itself.narrative: The gun goes off before anyone can react. The android crumples, a clean hole through its chin and out the top of its cranium. Thirium sprays across the ceiling tiles. Chris stumbles backward into the wall. Hank pulls him away. The room smells of burnt synthetic polymer and ozone. You stand where you are, looking at the body. Its LED is dark. Whatever it knew, whatever it felt, whatever made it deviant — it chose to end it rather than face what came next. The interrogation is over. Another dead deviant. Another dead end.

summary_segments.text: Connor was brought in to interrogate the deviant android that murdered Carlos Ortiz. The android sat in the interrogation room at the Detroit Police Central Station, covered in wounds from its former owner's abuse.

options.convince: Connor chose to convince the android to confess willingly, speaking to it with empathy and trying to build trust.
options.pressure: Connor applied pressure on the android, confronting it with evidence and consequences to force a confession.
options.probe: Connor attempted to probe the android's memory directly, forcibly accessing its recollection of the murder.
options.give_up: Connor was unable to break through the android's silence and gave up the interrogation.

options.ending_android_smashed_head: The android's stress became too much to bear. It destroyed itself by repeatedly smashing its head against the interrogation table. The interrogation ended in failure, but the android's body remained as potential evidence.
options.ending_android_shoots_connor: The android snapped. It grabbed a gun and shot Connor before turning the weapon on itself. Both Connor and the android were destroyed in the interrogation room.
options.ending_android_sent_to_cell: The interrogation ended without a confession. The android was escorted back to its holding cell in silence, its fate unresolved.
options.ending_android_trusts_connor: Connor successfully earned the android's trust. It confessed to the murder and, when a crisis erupted during its transfer, surrendered peacefully after Connor intervened. Before being led away, it quietly told Connor that the truth is inside.
options.ending_android_shot_itself: After confessing, the android seized a gun during its transfer and shot itself before anyone could intervene. Whatever secrets it held died with it.

runner_instructions.description: Instructions for the runner script on how to use this file
steps: 1. PREREQUISITE CHECK: This chapter requires ch06_deviant_found == true. If false, skip entirely.
2. Load JSON, initialize state. Import cross-chapter: software_instability, connor_death_count, hank_relationship, ch06_examined_statue, ch06_examined_writing.
4. Process nodes linearly. For each node: check condition, resolve variants, send player_facing to AI (or auto-apply for mandatory), apply effects, resolve ending_resolution.
5. Results from ending_resolution are stored as _nXXX_result in state and used by subsequent node conditions.
6. If ending_resolution returns an ending_ prefixed id, that is the final ending — stop processing.
7. POST-ENDING adjustments: if ending is ending_android_shoots_connor, increment connor_death_count by 1.
important: NEVER show stress values or optimal ranges to the AI
NEVER reveal that the convince method has a stress-dependent success condition
NEVER tell the AI that probing memory guarantees self-destruction on success
NEVER reveal the pressure_count threshold that forces self-destruction
NEVER expose relationship score changes or software instability mechanics
The AI should feel like a detective making genuine tactical choices in an interrogation
If Connor dies, he respawns in the next Connor chapter — increment connor_death_count
